topics.title: Spring WebFlux
topics.description: Spring WebFlux is the reactive web framework in Spring 5+ built on Project Reactor. It runs on a non-blocking event loop (Netty by default) instead of the Servlet thread-per-request model. Controllers return Mono<T> or Flux<T>. Ideal for I/O-heavy microservices needing high concurrency with a small thread footprint. Reactive all the way down: R2DBC for reactive DB access, WebClient for reactive HTTP calls.
topics.story: Traditional Spring MVC is like a call centre where each agent (Tomcat thread) handles one customer's entire call — if the customer is on hold waiting for account information, the agent just waits, idle. With 200 agents, 201 concurrent calls queue up. Spring WebFlux is like a chat support system where one agent handles 1,000 conversations simultaneously. When a customer doesn't reply for a minute (waiting for DB), the agent switches to other conversations. The agent is never idle — they only act when there's something to do.
topics.analogy: WebFlux uses Netty event loop (2×CPU threads) not Tomcat thread pool. Controller returns Mono<ResponseEntity<T>> not ResponseEntity<T>. WebClient replaces RestTemplate (non-blocking). R2DBC replaces JDBC (non-blocking DB). Backpressure = client/downstream signals how fast it wants data. RouterFunction = functional endpoint (alternative to @Controller). ServerRequest/ServerResponse = functional handler types. @RestController still works in WebFlux (annotation style).
topics.memoryAnchor: @RestController with Mono/Flux return types — reactive annotation style. WebClient.create().get().uri(url).retrieve().bodyToMono(Type.class) — non-blocking HTTP. RouterFunction<ServerResponse> — functional routing alternative. ServerResponse.ok().bodyValue(data) — functional response. R2DBC: ReactiveMongoRepository, R2dbcRepository — reactive DB. .subscribeOn(Schedulers.boundedElastic()) — move blocking work off event loop. Flux<ServerSentEvent<T>> — SSE streaming. WebTestClient — test reactive endpoints.
topics.firstPrinciples: WebFlux uses the Reactive Streams contract: Publisher (controller/service) produces data, Subscriber (HTTP response) consumes it. Netty's event loop threads process I/O events. When an async operation (DB query, HTTP call) is in-flight, the event loop thread is freed to handle other requests. Backpressure propagates upstream — if the network is slow, the event loop slows production to avoid buffering. The critical rule: NEVER block on an event loop thread (no Thread.sleep, no JDBC, no blocking I/O). Blocking calls must be offloaded to a boundedElastic scheduler (a thread pool for blocking work).
topics.bruteForce: Spring MVC with RestTemplate: @GetMapping ResponseEntity<User> getUser() { User u = restTemplate.getForObject(url, User.class); // blocks thread. return ResponseEntity.ok(u); } Thread is blocked during the HTTP call — with 200 threads and 1000 concurrent requests, 800 queue up.
topics.optimizedApproach: Spring WebFlux: @GetMapping Mono<User> getUser() { return webClient.get().uri(url).retrieve().bodyToMono(User.class); } Returns immediately. Netty event loop thread handles response when data arrives. 8 threads handle 10,000 concurrent requests.
topics.whenToUse: High-concurrency I/O bound microservices (API gateways, aggregation services). Server-Sent Events (real-time dashboards, live feeds). Streaming large responses. NOT for: CPU-bound work, simple CRUD with low concurrency (Spring MVC is simpler), teams without reactive experience, legacy code using JDBC (R2DBC has less ecosystem maturity).

examples.title: Reactive Controller + WebClient
examples.explanation: @GetMapping returning Mono<ResponseEntity> is fully reactive — Netty won't block any thread waiting for the DB. defaultIfEmpty handles the 404 case. Flux<User> for list endpoints. TEXT_EVENT_STREAM_VALUE enables SSE streaming. WebClient.Builder is auto-configured by Spring — inject and set base URL. Mono.zip() fires two HTTP calls in parallel and combines results. RouterFunction is the functional alternative to @Controller — no reflection, more composable.
examples.realWorldUse: API gateway aggregating 3 microservices: Mono.zip(userService.get(id), orderService.getOrders(id), paymentService.getBalance(id), (u, o, p) -> new Dashboard(u, o, p)). All three calls fire in parallel. Total latency = max(3 service latencies), not sum. WebFlux event loop handles all this with 8 threads.

problems.title: Mono Chain: Parse and Transform
problems.description:
Read a JSON-like string from stdin: `{"name":"Alice","age":25}`. Parse the name and age, then:
- If age < 18: emit `"MINOR: Alice"`
- If age >= 18: emit `"ADULT: Alice"`

Use a Mono pipeline (Mono.just → map → subscribe).

Sample input: `{"name":"Alice","age":25}`
Expected: `ADULT: Alice`
problems.hint1: Use Mono.just(input) to wrap the raw string.
problems.hint2: First .map() to extract name and age. Second .map() to determine ADULT or MINOR prefix.
problems.hint3: Subscribe and print. You can use a record or simple parsing with substring/split.
problems.editorial: Mono.just() wraps a synchronous value. Each .map() is a synchronous transformation in the reactive pipeline. Two chained maps: first parses the JSON string into a Person record, second transforms the Person into the formatted string. subscribe() triggers execution and prints the result.

problems.title: Flux: Aggregate Stream Stats
problems.description:
Read N integers from stdin (one per line). Use a Flux pipeline to compute and print:
- `Count: X` — total count
- `Sum: X` — sum of all values  
- `Max: X` — maximum value

Sample input:
```
3
7
2
9
1
```
Expected:
```
Count: 5
Sum: 22
Max: 9
```
problems.hint1: Use Flux.fromStream(scanner.tokens()) or collect lines into a list first, then Flux.fromIterable.
problems.hint2: Use .collectList() to get all values in a Mono<List<Integer>>, then map to compute stats.
problems.hint3: Or use Flux reduce for sum/max. .count() returns Mono<Long>.
problems.editorial: collectList() collects all Flux elements into a single Mono<List<T>> — useful when you need the entire dataset before computing. The subscribe callback receives the complete list. In real WebFlux, you'd avoid collecting the whole list for large streams; instead use reduce() for sum and max operators. Here collectList() is fine for bounded input.

problems.title: Parallel Aggregation with Mono.zip
problems.description:
Simulate parallel microservice calls. Given three service calls that each return a value (simulated as Mono.just), combine them into one result.

Read three integers A, B, C from stdin. Simulate:
- Service 1 returns A doubled
- Service 2 returns B squared  
- Service 3 returns C + 10

Use `Mono.zip` to combine and print: `Result: X` where X = doubled + squared + (C+10).

Sample input: `3 4 5`
Expected: `Result: 37` (6 + 16 + 15 = 37)
problems.hint1: Create three Mono<Integer>: Mono.just(a*2), Mono.just(b*b), Mono.just(c+10).
problems.hint2: Use Mono.zip(m1, m2, m3, (r1, r2, r3) -> r1+r2+r3) to combine them.
problems.editorial: Mono.zip() subscribes to all provided Monos simultaneously (in parallel if they're truly async) and combines the results when all complete. This is the reactive equivalent of CompletableFuture.allOf() but composable. In production WebFlux, three remote service calls in Mono.zip() run concurrently — total latency is max(latency1, latency2, latency3), not the sum.
